card.intuition: Map both nodes and keys onto a circular ring (hash ring). Each key is served by the first node clockwise from the key on the ring. When a node is added/removed, only O(K/n) keys need to be remapped — not all K keys.
card.analogy: Assigning customers to bank branches around a circular route: each customer goes to the nearest branch clockwise. Adding a new branch only steals customers from the next branch clockwise — all other assignments are unchanged.
card.time_complexity: O(log n) per key lookup with sorted node ring; O(1) amortised key migration per node change
when_to_use: Partitioning data across distributed storage nodes
Load balancing where node changes should minimise disruption
gotchas: Without virtual nodes, one physical node may hold far more keys than others — use k virtual nodes per physical node for uniform distribution
Virtual nodes are additional ring positions that map to the same physical node
The ring is typically modulo 2³² (32-bit hash space)

exercise.prompt: Implement consistent hashing: add/remove nodes, look up which node serves a key.
hints: If bisect_right returns an index equal to the list length, wrap to 0 using modulo: idx % len(self.sorted_keys)
exercise.test_code:
ch = ConsistentHash(vnodes=50)
ch.add_node('A')
ch.add_node('B')
ch.add_node('C')

# Each key maps to some node
nodes = set(ch.get_node(f'key{i}') for i in range(100))
assert nodes <= {'A','B','C'}
assert len(nodes) >= 2, 'Keys should distribute to multiple nodes'

# Remove a node — remaining keys still map to valid nodes
ch.remove_node('B')
for i in range(100):
    assert ch.get_node(f'key{i}') in {'A','C'}
print('PASS')
